title: Structural Obstructions to Integer-Graded Hard-Lefschetz Geometry in Noncommutative Spectral Models
summary: Noncommutative geometric realizations of the Weil explicit formula recover the arithmetic distributions of the zeta function, but they rely on regularization cutoffs that reflect noncompact scaling and do not yield the compact, integer-graded structures required for a Hard-Lefschetz proof. Quantum statistical mechanical models have Hamiltonians with logarithmic spectra, and attempts to extract an integer grading via cyclic or K-theoretic λ-operations fail in the surveyed settings. A general operator-theoretic analysis shows that a bounded Lefschetz operator satisfying [T,L]=cL is obstructed by the expected ℚ-linear independence of zero ordinates, and unbounded alternatives require infinitely many repeated gaps c that are not seen in high-precision zero statistics. Positivity statements obtained in this framework are equivalent to the Riemann Hypothesis, and an intersection theory on the square of the scaling/adele-class site sufficient to define a diagonal class and Hodge index form is not yet available.
background: The geometric class K_geom/SURF must support an integer-graded sl<sub>2</sub>-action with a compact-type Hodge-Riemann form, and a Lefschetz operator L that is independent of the spectral operator T extracted from the Weil explicit formula. Route C (Connes-Consani scaling/adele-class space and related quantum statistical mechanical models) gives a spectral realization of zeros and a trace-formula avatar of the explicit formula, but its scaling dynamics are inherently noncompact. This clashes with the need for a lowest-weight grading, compactness-like positivity, and a bona fide intersection theory on a "square" admitting a diagonal class [Δ]. The following synthesis delineates the precise mechanisms by which these requirements fail or remain unconstructed in the current noncommutative frameworks.
content:
Connes's program rigorously interprets the Weil explicit formula as a trace formula on the adèle class space, realizing the nontrivial zeros as an "absorption spectrum." However, this realization uses regularization cutoffs - phase-space projections, multiplicative windowing [λ<sup>-1</sup>,λ], and finite time/frequency truncations - reflecting the noncompact scaling dynamics. These cutoffs are adjustable and, as surveyed, no published work quantitatively reproduces global long-range signatures of the zeros such as the observed saturation of the number variance Δ<sub>3</sub>(L). In parallel, a full intersection theory on the "square" needed to define a diagonal class [Δ] and an associated Hodge-index pairing is not constructed, leaving the positivity and Lefschetz machinery without a geometric home inside this framework.

    Multiple, independent mechanisms block the emergence of an integer-graded Hard-Lefschetz structure. First, in the scaling-site geometry, each periodic orbit C_p=ℝ<sub>+*</sub>/p^ℤ has a Riemann-Roch formula χ(D)=deg(D) with deg(D) an arbitrary real number, making an integer-valued Euler characteristic - and hence a finite-dimensional, integer-graded cohomology - impossible on C_p. Second, in quantum statistical mechanical realizations (e.g., Bost-Connes and its GL<sub>2</sub> generalization), the time evolution is generated by a Hamiltonian with logarithmic discrete spectrum (eigenvalues like log n or log det(m)), which is incompatible with the integer spectrum required of the grading operator in a Hard-Lefschetz sl<sub>2</sub>-triple. Third, passing to cyclic or topological K-theory does not reveal a hidden integer grading: known cyclic λ-operations yielding eigenvalues m<sup>j</sup> apply to commutative settings and not to the noncommutative C*-algebras at hand, and no λ-ring/Adams package is established for these algebras' K-theory.

    A separate, operator-theoretic obstruction concerns compatibility with the spectral operator T. For a self-adjoint T with discrete spectrum, any bounded L satisfying [T,L]=cL must vanish unless the spectrum contains arithmetic progressions with step c; under the anticipated ℚ-linear independence of the zeros' ordinates, such progressions are absent. While unbounded L can satisfy [T,L]=cL on a dense domain even without arithmetic progressions, existence requires infinitely many spectral pairs with fixed gap c. High-precision statistics of the first 10<sup>6</sup> zeros show pair correlations matching GUE without spikes at any fixed c, ratio statistics revealing no excess of approximate 3-term arithmetic progressions, and a Δ<sub>3</sub>(L) curve that saturates at long range but not via repeated gap structures - all weighing strongly against the spectral repetitions needed for such an unbounded L tied directly to the zeros.

    Finally, positivity sources within the noncommutative adèle-class framework are equivalent to RH and thus cannot furnish an independent Hodge-Riemann form. Although the noncommutative trace formula sharply reproduces the arithmetic structure of the explicit formula (archimedean Ψ and von Mangoldt weights), it stops short of supplying a compact, integer-graded cohomology, an independent Lefschetz class, and a non-circular positivity theory on primitives. As things stand, Route C realizes the explicit formula but does not provide G2 (integer grading/Hard-Lefschetz) or G4 (independent Hodge-Riemann positivity), and it lacks the intersection theory necessary to define [Δ] with the required properties on a geometric "square."

findings.content: The Connes program provides a rigorous interpretation of the Weil explicit formula as a trace formula on the adèle class space. In this framework, the nontrivial zeros of the Riemann zeta function manifest as an "absorption spectrum" (missing spectral lines) [r0, connes2000noncommutativegeometryyear pages 55-58, connesUnknownyearformuledetrace pages 1-3]. This interpretation provides a spectral realization of the zeta zeros.
findings.caveat: A key feature of this framework is its reliance on regularization cutoffs. The trace formula expressions require regularization and their values depend on these cutoffs, which reflects the noncompact nature of the underlying scaling dynamics. These cutoffs appear in several forms: explicit phase-space projections (`P_Λ`, `P̂_Λ`), compression via a Sonin projection, multiplicative interval restrictions (`[λ⁻¹, λ]`), and finite time/frequency windows (`T`, `W`) used for semiclassical counting [r0, r65, r70, connes2000noncommutativegeometryyear pages 55-58, connes2602theriemannhypothesis pages 4-6, connes2021weilpositivityand pages 1-3, connes2019thescalinghamiltonian pages 5-11]. While these parameters could, in principle, suppress long-range correlations, no work has yet shown they quantitatively reproduce global statistical signatures like the observed saturation of the number variance `Δ₃(L)` [r65]. This presents a challenge for obtaining a theory from a simple compact geometry [r0, connes2000noncommutativegeometryyear pages 55-58, connes2602theriemannhypothesis pages 4-6]. In contrast, Arakelov theory (Route A) contains several intrinsic rigidity mechanisms that could plausibly lead to such saturation, even if a direct proof is lacking. These include exact cancellation/anomaly compensation in arithmetic Riemann-Roch theorems (where archimedean analytic torsion is balanced by algebraic terms), and the existence of deterministic leading terms with explicitly bounded subleading errors in arithmetic volume asymptotics [r70, gillet1992anarithmeticriemannroch pages 49-53, bismut1998localindextheory pages 1-3, boucksom2011okounkovbodiesof pages 16-20].

findings.content: An obstruction exists to directly obtaining an integer-graded cohomology from the geometry of the Connes-Consani scaling site. For the periodic orbits `C_p = R_+^*/p^Z` (compact quasi-tropical curves associated with each prime `p`), a Riemann-Roch theorem has been established. This theorem states that the Euler characteristic of a divisor, `χ(D)`, is equal to its degree, `deg(D)`, which can be any real number [r0, connes2017geometryofthe pages 3-6]. If a cohomology theory on `C_p` were composed of finite-dimensional spaces with integer dimensions, its Euler characteristic `χ(D)` would have to be an integer. The fact that `deg(D)` can be non-integral makes the established Riemann-Roch theorem incompatible with any such integer-graded cohomology theory on `C_p` [r0].

findings.content:
An analysis of the Connes program (Route C) and related spectral frameworks against the requirements for a geometric proof of the Riemann Hypothesis (RH) reveals several open problems, missing components, or necessary clarifications as of 2026 [r0, r4, r9, r11, r33, r40, r43, r48, r50, r54, r61]:
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            1.  **Intersection Theory:** A full intersection theory on the 'square' of the arithmetic/scaling site, which is needed to define a diagonal class `[Δ]` and its intersection number (for Hodge-index positivity), is not yet constructed [r0, r4, connes2016anessayon pages 18-21, connes2020theriemann–rochstrategy pages 19-22].
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            2.  **Operator Independence and Existence:** A candidate Lefschetz operator `L` derived from independent geometric data must satisfy `L ∉ W*(T)`. Furthermore, for an `sl₂`-structure, it is expected to satisfy a commutation relation `[T,L]=cL` for some `c≠0`. However, operator theory provides a nuanced obstruction to the existence of such an `L`.
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                *   For a self-adjoint operator `T` with a discrete spectrum, any *bounded* operator `L` satisfying `[T,L]=cL` can be non-zero only if the spectrum of `T` contains arithmetic progressions with step `c` [r9, albeverio2003graphsubspacesand pages 7-10]. If the Riemann zeros (`γ_ρ`) are, as conjectured, `Q`-linearly independent, then the spectrum of `T` contains no such arithmetic progressions for any `c`, forcing any bounded `L` to be zero.
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                *   For an *unbounded* operator `L`, the situation is more complex. The standard proof (using matrix elements between eigenvectors) breaks down because of operator domain issues. Rigorous functional analysis shows that the commutation relation `[T,L]v = cLv`, when formulated on a common dense domain, does not necessarily force arithmetic progressions in the spectrum of `T` [r43, bagarello2011weakcommutationrelations pages 1-4]. The condition for a non-zero solution `L` to exist is that the set of pairs of eigenvalues with a fixed difference `c`, `S_c = {(λ_m, λ_n) | λ_m - λ_n = c}`, must be non-empty. For `L` to be unbounded, this set must be infinite [r48]. This is a much weaker condition than the existence of arithmetic progressions.
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                *   An explicit, unconditional construction confirms this possibility. An operator `T` with a discrete spectrum `{λ_n}` explicitly lacking 3-term arithmetic progressions can be constructed (e.g., `λ_n` is the n-th element of the sorted set `{3^k, 3^k+1 | k ≥ 2}`). This spectrum has an infinite number of pairs with difference `c=1`. An unbounded, non-zero operator `L` can be defined on `ℓ²(ℕ)` such that `[T,L]v = 1·Lv` holds exactly on the dense domain of finite sequences. This demonstrates that the `[T,L]=cL` relation can coexist with a non-arithmetic-progression spectrum, provided `L` is unbounded [r48].
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                *   A proposed resolution is that the compatibility of the `sl₂` action with the spectral operator `T` is only possible if `L` acts on an auxiliary, integer-graded "Tate-twist" component of the cohomology, rather than directly shifting the spectrum of the zeros [r11, consani2006archimedeancohomologyrevisited pages 3-5, consani2006archimedeancohomologyrevisited pages 21-24].
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            3.  **Positivity Circularity:** The known positivity conditions within the Connes framework, such as the positivity of the Weil distribution for certain test functions, are rigorously shown to be equivalent to RH itself. Therefore, they cannot be used as an independent source of positivity to prove the hypothesis [r0, r4, connes2016anessayon pages 8-11].
findings.caveat:
The operator-theoretic obstruction described in point (2) is heavily dependent on the properties of `L`. The obstruction for *bounded* operators is strong, but an analysis of the Arakelov geometry framework (Route A) reveals that the candidate Lefschetz operator `L = ∪ĉ₁` involves differential operators and is thus expected to be *unbounded* [r40, menares2011correspondencesinarakelov pages 16-18, bruinier2007borcherdsproductsand pages 10-13]. The successful construction of an explicit example of an unbounded `L` satisfying `[T,L]=cL` for a `T` with a non-arithmetic spectrum confirms that this is a mathematically viable path [r48]. However, high-precision numerical analyses of the Riemann zero spectrum provide strong evidence against the existence of the necessary arithmetic structures.
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                *   An analysis of the first 1,000,000 nontrivial Riemann zeros finds that the distribution of gaps is statistically indistinguishable from the smooth pair-correlation function predicted by the Gaussian Unitary Ensemble (GUE) model, with no significant spikes at any specific gap value `c` [r50]. This suggests that the spectrum does not contain the infinitely many repeated gaps required for the existence of an unbounded operator `L` satisfying `[T,L]=cL` [r48, r50].
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                *   A subsequent analysis of 3-point correlations in the same dataset of 1,000,000 zeros also finds no evidence for hidden arithmetic structures. The distribution of the ratio of consecutive unfolded gaps, `r_n = (γ_{n+2}−γ_{n+1})/(γ_{n+1}−γ_n)`, agrees well with GUE predictions. The only statistically significant deviations are a small *deficit* (not an excess) of zeros at small ratios (`r` ≈ 0.09–0.25), indicating the zeros are slightly more rigid than finite-N GUE predictions. There is no excess of approximate 3-term arithmetic progressions (which would appear near `r=1`), further undermining the idea that the spectrum possesses local arithmetic patterns that could be exploited by a simple operator algebra [r54].
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                *   A global analysis of the number variance statistic `Δ₃(L)` for the first 1,000,000 unfolded Riemann zeros provides a sharp quantitative constraint. The statistic matches GUE predictions for short-range correlations (`L` ≲ 4) but exhibits a sharp saturation for long-range correlations. A precise analysis shows the empirical `Δ₃(L)` breaks from the GUE prediction at `L ≈ 4-5` and settles onto a plateau with a height of `Δ₃^∞ ≈ 0.1908 ± 0.0001` [r61, r69]. The onset of this saturation occurs at `L ≈ 76` [r69]. This saturation is quantitatively predicted by the semiclassical theory of quantum chaos based on the prime-sum component of the explicit formula [r74, berry1988semiclassicalformulafor]. The predicted asymptotic value is given by `Δ₃^∞ ≈ (1/2) * Σ²_∞(E) = (1/2π²) * [ln ln(E/2π) + C]`, where `E` is the energy (height of the highest zero), and `C ≈ 1.4009` is a constant derived from a prime sum. For the dataset of 10⁶ zeros (with `E ≈ 6×10⁵`), this formula yields a predicted plateau of `≈ 0.1946`, which agrees with the empirical value `≈ 0.1908` to within 2% [r74]. This provides a sharp quantitative target: any geometric model for the zeros must reproduce not only the saturation but also its specific numerical value and its `ln ln(E)` scaling. A survey of the noncommutative geometry literature confirms that while its trace formula framework has regularization parameters that could affect long-range correlations, no published work has demonstrated that they quantitatively reproduce the observed saturation value or its scaling [r65]. In contrast, Arakelov theory contains intrinsic rigidity mechanisms (e.g., anomaly compensation, bounded errors in asymptotics) that provide a plausible, though not yet demonstrated, path to explaining this saturation [r70].

findings.content: A fundamental obstruction to realizing an integer-graded `sl₂`-structure within quantum statistical mechanical (QSM) models for number theory (a component of Route C) has been identified. In the Bost-Connes system and its `GL₂` generalization, the canonical time evolution `σ_t` is implemented by a Hamiltonian operator `H` whose spectrum is discrete but logarithmic, consisting of eigenvalues like `log n` or `log det(m)` [r3, connes2004fromphysicsto pages 19-23, connes2006kmsstatesand pages 12-14]. This is structurally incompatible with the requirement for a grading operator in a Hard-Lefschetz triple to have an integer spectrum, a condition necessary for constructing a lowest-weight representation and applying Hodge-Riemann-type positivity arguments [r3].

findings.content: The obstruction to obtaining an integer grading in the Bost-Connes system (Route C), identified at the level of the Hamiltonian's logarithmic spectrum, is not resolved by passing to a homological framework. A literature review on the cyclic homology of the Bost-Connes C*-algebra (`A`) shows that while explicit computations of the homology groups (`HH_n(A)`, `HC_n(A)`) exist for a dense subalgebra, the action of the time-evolution flow `σ_t` on these groups is not known to induce a decomposition into integer-weight eigenspaces [r8, leichtnam2000crossedproductalgebras pages 4-8, connes2007noncommutativegeometryand pages 3-5]. A separate framework for cyclic homology involving Connes-style `λ`-operations is known to produce integer-weight decompositions with eigenvalues `m^j` for *commutative* algebras, but the surveyed literature does not show this framework is applicable to the non-commutative Bost-Connes algebra or connect it to the `σ_t` flow [r8, r28, connes2014cyclichomologyserres pages 5-8, connes2015thecyclicand pages 34-35]. Therefore, the search for a "hidden" integer grading at the cohomological level remains unsuccessful [r8, r28].

findings.content:
Attempts to import a canonical integer grading into the operator-algebraic frameworks of Route C (e.g., the Bost-Connes system) via `λ`-ring structures have not been successful. A systematic literature review confirms two distinct failures:
                                                                                                                                                                        1.  On the level of topological K-theory, a `λ`-ring structure and associated Adams operations `ψ^n` have not been established for the C*-algebras `A_K` of the Bost-Connes system or its common generalizations. While semigroup-induced endomorphisms exist on K-theory in some models, they are not presented as part of a `λ`-ring structure [r28, cuntz2010c*algebrasassociatedwith pages 29-31].
                                                                                                                                                                        2.  On the level of cyclic homology, the established Connes-style `λ`-operations that provide a canonical integer-weight decomposition (with eigenvalues `m^j`) are proven for commutative algebras or specific epicyclic modules. The literature does not show that the non-commutative C*-algebras `A_K` or their smooth subalgebras meet the necessary conditions for this decomposition to hold [r28, connes2014cyclichomologyserres pages 5-8, connes2015thecyclicand pages 34-35].
                                                                                                                                                                        This confirms that the standard algebraic mechanisms for producing integer gradings are not currently available within the primary non-commutative frameworks used to model the zeta function's spectrum.

findings.content:
A survey of Arakelov and noncommutative geometry frameworks reveals that the most explicit geometric realization of the Weil explicit formula is found in the noncommutative setting.
                                                                                                            1. **Arakelov Riemann-Roch:** Canonical arithmetic Riemann-Roch (ARR) theorems in Arakelov geometry (e.g., Gillet-Soulé) provide global-to-local formulas that are structurally analogous to the Weil explicit formula but are not equivalent to it [r45, r49]. Both frameworks separate contributions from finite and infinite places. However, a direct transformation of the ARR identity into the Weil formula is obstructed by key differences [r49]:
                                                                                                               * **Formalism Mismatch:** ARR theorems typically result in an equality of real numbers or arithmetic classes (e.g., the arithmetic Euler characteristic), whereas the Weil formula is an identity between distributions acting on a space of test functions [r49, gillet1992anarithmeticriemannroch pages 53-56].
                                                                                                               * **Finite Place Mismatch:** The finite-place contribution in ARR is a sum over closed points `x` of an arithmetic scheme, with terms weighted by `log(#k(x))`, where `k(x)` is the residue field. For a point over a prime `p` with residue degree `m`, this weight is `m log(p)`. In contrast, the corresponding term in the Weil formula involves the von Mangoldt function `Λ(p^m) = log(p)` [r49, r68, gillet1992anarithmeticriemannroch pages 53-56, connes1999traceformulain pages 69-73]. This mismatch in weighting is a fundamental obstacle. While Deninger's dynamical systems framework provides a conceptual reinterpretation where closed points correspond to periodic orbits, the natural length of an orbit corresponding to a point with residue degree `m` remains `m log(p)`, thus not resolving the mismatch [r68, r77, deninger2006adynamicalsystems pages 4-8]. The noncommutative framework of Connes provides a more successful resolution by counting primitive periodic orbits (indexed by primes `p`) of length `log p`, where prime powers `p^m` correspond to `m`-th iterations. A trace formula over such a system naturally produces a weight of `log p` for any contribution from the orbit associated with `p`, matching the von Mangoldt function's structure [r51, r55, r68, r77, connes2011thebcsystemand pages 28-31, connes2016anessayon pages 6-8].
                                                                                                               * **Archimedean Mismatch:** The archimedean component of ARR involves analytic torsion (a regularized determinant of Laplacians) and special values/derivatives of zeta functions via the `R`-genus [r49, gillet1992anarithmeticriemannroch pages 49-53]. While conceptually related to the gamma factor and `Ψ`-distribution terms in the Weil formula, the literature surveyed does not provide an explicit calculation that equates the ARR archimedean terms with the Weil distributions for a specific choice of arithmetic model and line bundle [r49].
                                                                                                               * **Lack of Numerical Identity:** The conceptual gap between Arakelov theory and dynamical trace formulas is reinforced by the lack of a proven numerical identity between their outputs. A survey of the literature failed to find a calculation showing that a fundamental Arakelov invariant, such as the arithmetic self-intersection number `deg(ĉ₁(O(1))²)` on `P¹_ℤ`, is numerically equal to a value derived from a Deninger-style trace formula over prime periodic orbits [r73]. This suggests the analogy between the two frameworks has not yet been elevated to a direct equivalence.
                                                                                                            2. **Noncommutative Trace Formula:** In contrast, the Connes-Consani framework realizes the Weil explicit formula directly as a Lefschetz-type trace formula on the cohomology of the adèle class space. This formulation successfully recovers the terms of the explicit formula from a geometric object. The "geometric side" of the trace formula is a sum of local distributions, given by principal-value integrals over the local multiplicative groups `ℚ_ν^×` for all places `ν ≤ ∞`. This directly corresponds to the structure of the Weil explicit formula [r45, r68, connes2010theweilproof pages 26-30, khalkhali2008aninvitationto pages 95-98]. Specifically, the archimedean term `Ψ(r)` is explicitly recovered from a distribution built with the kernel `-log|t|`, and the combination of finite-place terms corresponds to the sum over prime powers involving the von Mangoldt function, realized via periodic orbits of length `log p` [r45, r68, connes2021weilpositivityand pages 10-12, burnol1998theexplicitformula pages 13-16, connes2401knotsprimesand pages 1-4]. However, this realization is not yet a complete geometric proof in the sense of Grothendieck-Lefschetz; it is an explicit recovery of the distributional formula, but a full cohomology theory `H*(S)` for the scaling site `S` upon which a Frobenius correspondence `F*` would act is still considered an open problem, pending the development of a suitable intersection theory [r55, connes2016anessayon pages 18-21, connes2020theriemann–rochstrategy pages 19-22].
                                                                                                            This demonstrates that while classical Arakelov theory provides a geometric framework with the correct global-to-local structure, the noncommutative and dynamical systems approaches currently provide a more direct and explicit derivation of the Weil formula's arithmetic terms as a geometric trace formula [r45, r49, r51, r55, r68, r73, r77].

findings.content:
The noncommutative geometry framework (Route C) provides analogues for Arakelov geometry components, but a formal, complete dictionary relating the two remains unestablished, and NCG lacks an independent source of positivity comparable to the arithmetic Hodge Index theorem [r56, r58, r63].
                                                                                                                                                                        1.  **Candidate "Intersection Products" & "Degree Maps":** A conceptual bridge exists where "integration" in both theories is realized via regularized traces or determinants. Arakelov geometry uses analytic torsion and zeta-regularized determinants for its Archimedean contributions [r58, gillet1991analytictorsionand pages 20-23], while NCG uses functionals like the Dixmier trace and the Connes-Moscovici residue cocycle [r56, connes2000ashortsurvey pages 10-13]. At the Archimedean places, an explicit link exists: Archimedean L-factors can be recovered from the regularized determinants of spectral triples [r58, consani2002newperspectivesin pages 9-11]. However, a survey of the literature did not find a general theorem that equates the full Gillet-Soulé arithmetic intersection pairing `deg(a·b)` with a specific NCG trace pairing `Tr(φ(a)ψ(b))`, nor a realization of arithmetic correspondences as KK-elements [r58]. A targeted search for such a formula for the archimedean component of the intersection pairing confirmed this gap: the literature does not contain an explicit formula that equates the Arakelov-Green's function integral `∫ g_y dμ_x` with the noncommutative residue `Res(Φ_x, Φ_y)` of pseudodifferential operators associated with the divisors [r63].
                                                                                                                                                                        2.  **Positivity Circularity:** Despite the existence of these tools, the most explicit positivity condition formulated within the adèle class space framework is shown to be equivalent to the Riemann Hypothesis itself. The condition that a specific trace pairing is positive, `Tr(ϑ(f ⋆ f♯)|H1) ≥ 0`, is not an independent input but rather a reformulation of the hypothesis [r56, connes2010theweilproof pages 26-30, khalkhali2008aninvitationto pages 95-98].
                                                                                                                                                                        This distinguishes Route C from Route A (Arakelov geometry), where proven Hodge Index theorems provide a source of sign-definiteness that is not conditional on RH (see `f11`). In the noncommutative framework, such an independent, unconditional positivity theorem on a primitive subspace has not been found [r56, r58].

    <title>Structural Obstructions to Integer-Graded Hard-Lefschetz Geometry in Noncommutative Spectral Models</title>
    <summary>Noncommutative geometric realizations of the Weil explicit formula recover the arithmetic distributions of the zeta function, but they rely on regularization cutoffs that reflect noncompact scaling and do not yield the compact, integer-graded structures required for a Hard-Lefschetz proof. Quantum statistical mechanical models have Hamiltonians with logarithmic spectra, and attempts to extract an integer grading via cyclic or K-theoretic λ-operations fail in the surveyed settings. A general operator-theoretic analysis shows that a bounded Lefschetz operator satisfying [T,L]=cL is obstructed by the expected ℚ-linear independence of zero ordinates, and unbounded alternatives require infinitely many repeated gaps c that are not seen in high-precision zero statistics. Positivity statements obtained in this framework are equivalent to the Riemann Hypothesis, and an intersection theory on the square of the scaling/adele-class site sufficient to define a diagonal class and Hodge index form is not yet available.</summary>
    <background>The geometric class K_geom/SURF must support an integer-graded sl<sub>2</sub>-action with a compact-type Hodge-Riemann form, and a Lefschetz operator L that is independent of the spectral operator T extracted from the Weil explicit formula. Route C (Connes-Consani scaling/adele-class space and related quantum statistical mechanical models) gives a spectral realization of zeros and a trace-formula avatar of the explicit formula, but its scaling dynamics are inherently noncompact. This clashes with the need for a lowest-weight grading, compactness-like positivity, and a bona fide intersection theory on a "square" admitting a diagonal class [Δ]. The following synthesis delineates the precise mechanisms by which these requirements fail or remain unconstructed in the current noncommutative frameworks.</background>
    <content>
    Connes's program rigorously interprets the Weil explicit formula as a trace formula on the adèle class space, realizing the nontrivial zeros as an "absorption spectrum." However, this realization uses regularization cutoffs - phase-space projections, multiplicative windowing [λ<sup>-1</sup>,λ], and finite time/frequency truncations - reflecting the noncompact scaling dynamics. These cutoffs are adjustable and, as surveyed, no published work quantitatively reproduces global long-range signatures of the zeros such as the observed saturation of the number variance Δ<sub>3</sub>(L). In parallel, a full intersection theory on the "square" needed to define a diagonal class [Δ] and an associated Hodge-index pairing is not constructed, leaving the positivity and Lefschetz machinery without a geometric home inside this framework.

    Multiple, independent mechanisms block the emergence of an integer-graded Hard-Lefschetz structure. First, in the scaling-site geometry, each periodic orbit C_p=ℝ<sub>+*</sub>/p^ℤ has a Riemann-Roch formula χ(D)=deg(D) with deg(D) an arbitrary real number, making an integer-valued Euler characteristic - and hence a finite-dimensional, integer-graded cohomology - impossible on C_p. Second, in quantum statistical mechanical realizations (e.g., Bost-Connes and its GL<sub>2</sub> generalization), the time evolution is generated by a Hamiltonian with logarithmic discrete spectrum (eigenvalues like log n or log det(m)), which is incompatible with the integer spectrum required of the grading operator in a Hard-Lefschetz sl<sub>2</sub>-triple. Third, passing to cyclic or topological K-theory does not reveal a hidden integer grading: known cyclic λ-operations yielding eigenvalues m<sup>j</sup> apply to commutative settings and not to the noncommutative C*-algebras at hand, and no λ-ring/Adams package is established for these algebras' K-theory.

    A separate, operator-theoretic obstruction concerns compatibility with the spectral operator T. For a self-adjoint T with discrete spectrum, any bounded L satisfying [T,L]=cL must vanish unless the spectrum contains arithmetic progressions with step c; under the anticipated ℚ-linear independence of the zeros' ordinates, such progressions are absent. While unbounded L can satisfy [T,L]=cL on a dense domain even without arithmetic progressions, existence requires infinitely many spectral pairs with fixed gap c. High-precision statistics of the first 10<sup>6</sup> zeros show pair correlations matching GUE without spikes at any fixed c, ratio statistics revealing no excess of approximate 3-term arithmetic progressions, and a Δ<sub>3</sub>(L) curve that saturates at long range but not via repeated gap structures - all weighing strongly against the spectral repetitions needed for such an unbounded L tied directly to the zeros.

    Finally, positivity sources within the noncommutative adèle-class framework are equivalent to RH and thus cannot furnish an independent Hodge-Riemann form. Although the noncommutative trace formula sharply reproduces the arithmetic structure of the explicit formula (archimedean Ψ and von Mangoldt weights), it stops short of supplying a compact, integer-graded cohomology, an independent Lefschetz class, and a non-circular positivity theory on primitives. As things stand, Route C realizes the explicit formula but does not provide G2 (integer grading/Hard-Lefschetz) or G4 (independent Hodge-Riemann positivity), and it lacks the intersection theory necessary to define [Δ] with the required properties on a geometric "square."</content>
    <finding-sources>1, 2, 3, 6, 7, 9, 14, 16</finding-sources>
  </group>

title: Arakelov-λ-Ring Blueprint for K_geom: Independent Lefschetz Class and Non-Circular Positivity with Open sl
summary: Arakelov geometry supplies a canonical candidate for the Lefschetz operator L as cup-product with an arithmetic first Chern class, and a bilinear form Q given by arithmetic intersection followed by degree, together yielding a Hodge-Riemann sign via proven arithmetic Hodge index theorems. A metric-variation argument establishes structural independence of L from the spectral operator T: changing the hermitian metric alters L while T, determined by the zero set, remains fixed, implying L ∉ W*(T). λ-ring/Adams operations contribute an intrinsic integer grading, and in Arakelov motivic cohomology L shifts the Adams weight compatibly; however, the sl<sub>2</sub> grading operator aligns with cohomological degree, not Adams weight, and the absolute surface itself is not yet constructed. A geometric Λ (lowering operator) is missing in general (except for abelian varieties), but compatibility patterns such as [L, Hecke]=0 and known tensor-factor decouplings indicate a viable path to coexistence with spectral operators without violating linear-independence constraints.
background: Route A seeks K_geom/SURF inside Arakelov-type or 𝔽<sub>1</sub>/λ-ring architectures, aiming for a cohomology with integer grading, a geometric Lefschetz class [Δ], and a positive Hodge-Riemann form independent of RH. Arithmetic Chow and motivic theories deliver canonical intersection pairings and arithmetic Chern classes, while λ-ring structures furnish Adams operations ψ<sup>n</sup> that generate an integer weight decomposition. The open challenge is to assemble a true sl<sub>2</sub>-triple (L,Λ,H) acting on the appropriate arithmetic cohomology of an "absolute surface," with Λ realized geometrically and H giving an integer, lowest-weight grading. This group synthesizes the pieces that already satisfy core gates (independence, positivity) and identifies what remains open.
content:
Arakelov theory identifies the natural bilinear form Q as the arithmetic intersection pairing followed by degree, and the Lefschetz operator as L(x)=x⋅ĉ<sub>1</sub>(H̄) for an arithmetically ample hermitian line bundle. The complex structure J can be implemented by the archimedean Weil operator C acting on (p,q)-forms, so that defining ω(x,y)=−deg(C(L(x))·y) produces positivity on primitive classes. This is a non-circular source of sign-definiteness: for arithmetic divisors, the relevant (1,1)-form component makes C act as the identity and the arithmetic Hodge Index theorem gives −deg((L(x))·x)≥0 on primitives. Thus, Arakelov geometry supplies G4-type positivity without presupposing RH.

    Independence of L from T is certified by metric variation. The arithmetic first Chern class depends on the archimedean metric via a Green's function term −log∥s∥<sup>2</sup>, so varying the metric m deforms L to L_m while the spectral operator T, defined by the set of nontrivial zeros {γ_ρ}, is invariant. The difference ĉ<sub>1</sub>(m<sub>1</sub>)−ĉ<sub>1</sub>(m<sub>0</sub>) is a Bott-Chern secondary class and for P<sup>1</sup>_ℤ with O(1), the rescaling m<sub>1</sub>=e<sup>−c</sup>m<sub>0</sub> yields a nontrivial class whose intersection with a rational point like [0] is nonzero. Because W*(T) is fixed by {γ_ρ} and L_m varies with m, this furnishes strong evidence that L ∉ W*(T), passing the independence gate.

    λ-ring structures provide an intrinsic integer grading via Adams operations ψ<sup>n</sup>, which act diagonally with eigenvalues n<sup>j</sup> on weight-j components after rationalization. In Arakelov motivic cohomology, L=∪ĉ<sub>1</sub> shifts degree by +2 and Adams weight by +1, supplying a compatible bigrading and a concrete raising operator. However, the grading operator H in an sl<sub>2</sub>-triple is determined by cohomological degree (e.g., 2p−d), not by Adams weight, so identifying H with the Adams-weight operator is obstructed. Moreover, the sought "absolute surface" Spec ℤx<sub>𝔽<sub>1</sub></sub>Spec ℤ with a diagonal class [Δ] remains unconstructed, and adjacent 𝔽<sub>1</sub>-models that give spectral spaces (e.g., H<sup>1</sup>(P<sup>1</sup>_{𝔽<sub>1</sub>},Ω)) do not provide the required sl<sub>2</sub>-triple; related metaplectic/prolate sl<sub>2</sub> realizations have continuous spectra for the scaling generator, not the discrete lowest-weight grading needed.

    The lowering operator Λ is not generally realized as a geometric adjoint of L in arithmetic settings; instead, Λ and H are typically imposed axiomatically to secure integrality. A notable exception occurs for abelian varieties, where a Fourier-Mukai mechanism (with Poincaré bundle kernel) yields a bona fide geometric sl<sub>2</sub> with adjointness, but this relies on structures absent for Spec ℤ. Partial geometric progress exists in derived Hecke/correspondence algebras, where explicit degree-raising operators are constructed by pull-cup-push, though a full (e,f,h) triple is not yet assembled. Crucially, decoupled compatibility is available: on Shimura-type geometries, L commutes with Hecke operators because the automorphic hermitian metric is preserved under Hecke correspondences, and, more generally, tensor-factor models show how an sl<sub>2</sub> on one factor can coexist with an independent spectral action on another. Together, these features chart a rigorous blueprint that passes independence and non-circular positivity, isolates the missing Λ/[Δ]/surface pieces, and suggests a concrete path to G1-G4 once the absolute surface is built.

findings.content: A survey of 𝔽₁-geometry and λ-ring frameworks (Route A) reveals that they do not currently contain a constructed "absolute surface" (`Spec ℤ ×_{𝔽₁} Spec ℤ`) equipped with the necessary structures for a geometric proof of RH. The literature shows the absolute surface is a central motivation, but its construction as a scheme or similar object with a well-defined integer-graded cohomology and a diagonal class `[Δ]` is an open problem [r1, r7, bruyn2013absolutegeometryand pages 1-4, borger2009lambdaringsandthe pages 1-3]. A specific obstruction to constructing a Lefschetz-type raising operator `L` for `Spec ℤ` within the standard motivic cohomology framework has been identified: the motivic cohomology `H^p_M(Spec k, ℚ(q))` for a number field `k` is known to be zero for `p ≠ 1`. A Lefschetz operator realized as a cup product with a degree-2 cycle class would need non-zero cohomology groups in multiple degrees to act upon, a condition not met by `Spec ℤ` [r21, deninger1991lfunctionsandarithmetic pages 1-4].
findings.caveat: While a full theory for the absolute surface is missing, partial and analogous constructions exist that possess some of the required features. Connes and Consani have defined a sheaf `Ω` on the 𝔽₁-projective line `P¹_{𝔽₁}` whose first sheaf cohomology group, `H¹(P¹_{𝔽₁}, Ω)`, provides a spectral realization of the zeros of L-functions [r1, connes2010schemesover𝔽1 pages 3-5, connes2010schemesover𝔽1 pages 30-32]. This cohomology space is equipped with intrinsic endomorphisms that are candidates for a Lefschetz-like structure. However, a detailed analysis shows that these operators do not satisfy the commutation relations required to form an `sl₂`-triple [r10]. An adjacent "prolate operator" framework, developed by Connes, Consani, and Moscovici, does realize an explicit `sl₂(ℝ)` action via the metaplectic representation [r14, r34, connes2024zetazerosand pages 24-27]. In this framework, the generators are explicitly defined differential operators (e.g., `ϖ(h)=x∂x+1/2`, `ϖ(e_+)=iπx²`) and the prolate operator `Wλ` can be expressed as an element `h² + 4πλ²k - 1/4` in the universal enveloping algebra `U(sl₂)`. However, this `sl₂` action is not of the required type for a Hard Lefschetz/Hodge-Riemann proof of RH. Specifically, the non-compact Cartan/scaling generator `H` (or its self-adjoint version `S`) has a continuous spectrum, modeled by multiplication by a real variable `s` on `L²(ℝ)`. The integer and discrete spectra that appear in the model belong to different operators: a "number operator" `N` (related to polynomial degree) and the compact generator `k` (the Hermite operator) [r15, r34, connes2024zetazerosand pages 10-12, connes2024zetazerosand pages 24-27]. The `sl₂` action on `L²(ℝ)` decomposes into lowest-weight representations characterized by the discrete spectrum of the *compact* generator `k`, not the non-compact scaling generator `H`. A detailed investigation confirmed that the Riemann zeros are spectrally realized through operators constructed from the non-compact scaling generator (e.g., `Δ=H(1+H)`) or the full prolate operator `Wλ` which couples the scaling and compact generators. The literature does not support identifying the zeros with the discrete spectrum of the compact generator `k` itself [r34, connes2207hochschildhomologytrace pages 8-10, connes2023bcsystemabsolutecyclotomy pages 18-20, connes2112prolatespheroidaloperator pages 1-3]. This confirms that this specific `sl₂` realization does not provide the necessary integer-graded structure on the operator whose spectrum relates to the zeta zeros.

A survey of 𝔽₁-geometry and λ-ring frameworks (Route A) reveals that they do not currently contain a constructed "absolute surface" (`Spec ℤ ×_{𝔽₁} Spec ℤ`) equipped with the necessary structures for a geometric proof of RH. The literature shows the absolute surface is a central motivation, but its construction as a scheme or similar object with a well-defined integer-graded cohomology and a diagonal class `[Δ]` is an open problem [r1, r7, bruyn2013absolutegeometryand pages 1-4, borger2009lambdaringsandthe pages 1-3]. A specific obstruction to constructing a Lefschetz-type raising operator `L` for `Spec ℤ` within the standard motivic cohomology framework has been identified: the motivic cohomology `H^p_M(Spec k, ℚ(q))` for a number field `k` is known to be zero for `p ≠ 1`. A Lefschetz operator realized as a cup product with a degree-2 cycle class would need non-zero cohomology groups in multiple degrees to act upon, a condition not met by `Spec ℤ` [r21, deninger1991lfunctionsandarithmetic pages 1-4].
    <caveat>While a full theory for the absolute surface is missing, partial and analogous constructions exist that possess some of the required features. Connes and Consani have defined a sheaf `Ω` on the 𝔽₁-projective line `P¹_{𝔽₁}` whose first sheaf cohomology group, `H¹(P¹_{𝔽₁}, Ω)`, provides a spectral realization of the zeros of L-functions [r1, connes2010schemesover𝔽1 pages 3-5, connes2010schemesover𝔽1 pages 30-32]. This cohomology space is equipped with intrinsic endomorphisms that are candidates for a Lefschetz-like structure. However, a detailed analysis shows that these operators do not satisfy the commutation relations required to form an `sl₂`-triple [r10]. An adjacent "prolate operator" framework, developed by Connes, Consani, and Moscovici, does realize an explicit `sl₂(ℝ)` action via the metaplectic representation [r14, r34, connes2024zetazerosand pages 24-27]. In this framework, the generators are explicitly defined differential operators (e.g., `ϖ(h)=x∂x+1/2`, `ϖ(e_+)=iπx²`) and the prolate operator `Wλ` can be expressed as an element `h² + 4πλ²k - 1/4` in the universal enveloping algebra `U(sl₂)`. However, this `sl₂` action is not of the required type for a Hard Lefschetz/Hodge-Riemann proof of RH. Specifically, the non-compact Cartan/scaling generator `H` (or its self-adjoint version `S`) has a continuous spectrum, modeled by multiplication by a real variable `s` on `L²(ℝ)`. The integer and discrete spectra that appear in the model belong to different operators: a "number operator" `N` (related to polynomial degree) and the compact generator `k` (the Hermite operator) [r15, r34, connes2024zetazerosand pages 10-12, connes2024zetazerosand pages 24-27]. The `sl₂` action on `L²(ℝ)` decomposes into lowest-weight representations characterized by the discrete spectrum of the *compact* generator `k`, not the non-compact scaling generator `H`. findings.content:
The `ℕ_{>0}` monoid action arising from `λ`-ring structures (a component of Route A) provides a canonical source of integer grading for K-theory and motivic cohomology over `Spec ℤ`, but it does not appear to provide all necessary components for a geometric proof of RH [r16, r17, r20, r25, r26, r29, r30, r37, r44, r53, r59].
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            1.  **Integer Grading:** In `λ`-ring settings, the Adams operations `{ψ^n | n ∈ ℕ_{>0}}` are commuting endomorphisms. After rationalization, they often admit a simultaneous decomposition into eigenspaces (known as weight spaces) where `ψ^n` acts as multiplication by `n^j` on the weight-`j` component. This provides a canonical integer-based grading that is definitionally independent of the Riemann zero spectrum [r16, r17, hiller1981λringsandalgebraic pages 8-12, walker2020adamsoperationsin pages 113-116].
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            2.  **`sl₂` Structure and Compatibility:** A literature survey found no instances where an Adams operator itself (e.g., `ψ²`) acts as a Lefschetz-type raising/lowering operator in an `sl₂`-triple. The `ψ^n` operators act diagonally on the weight spaces, which is characteristic of a grading operator `H`, not a nilpotent raising operator `L` [r17, grayson1994weightfiltrationsin pages 10-12]. However, in certain arithmetic-geometric contexts, this Adams-weight grading is compatible with `sl₂` actions constructed from other means.
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                *   **Arakelov Motivic Cohomology:** Within this framework, the cup product with the arithmetic first Chern class of a metrized line bundle, `∪ ĉ₁`, acts as a raising operator. A detailed analysis confirms this operator `L = ∪ ĉ₁` is expected to define a map `H^p_{M,ar}(X, ℚ(q)) → H^{p+2}_{M,ar}(X, ℚ(q+1))`, shifting the cohomological degree `p` by +2 and the Adams weight `q` by +1 [r25, r37, scholbach2012arakelovmotiviccohomology pages 17-21, holmstrom2010arakelovmotiviccohomology pages 12-16]. This makes `L` compatible with the integer grading provided by the Adams weight. Furthermore, the Adams operations `ψ^k` act on the weight-`q` component by multiplication by `k^q`, providing a family of operators that depend only on the weight index `q` [r37, scholbach2012arakelovmotiviccohomology pages 14-17]. This bigraded structure is a plausible candidate for the "decoupled compatibility" model (see `f12`), where an `sl₂` action on the `p`-graded component could coexist with a spectral action on the `q`-graded component.
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                *   **Arithmetic Varieties:** For an abelian variety `A`, the geometric `sl₂` action on its Chow group is compatible with the Adams-weight grading (or the related Beauville grading). The Lefschetz operator `L` (cup-product with a polarization `θ`) maps `CH^p_s(A)` to `CH^{p+1}_s(A)`, preserving the Beauville index `s` [r20, r29, beauville2008theactionof pages 8-12, polishchuk2008fourierstablesubringsin pages 1-3].
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            3.  **Grading Operator Mismatch and Obstruction:** A key obstruction remains: in the `sl₂` formalisms developed for arithmetic varieties, the grading operator `H` is defined via the commutator `[Λ, L]`, and its eigenvalues are determined by the cohomological degree (e.g., `d+1-2p`), not by the Adams weight `q` [r26, r30, kresch2001standardconjecturesfor pages 1-3]. This structure has been made explicit in an axiomatic "Lefschetz-module" framework motivated by arithmetic Chow theory, where the lowering operator `Λ` is defined such that the commutator `H = [L, Λ]` acts by multiplication with the integer `2i-n` on the `i`-th graded piece `V_i` [r44, zhang2020standardconjecturesand pages 36-40]. This confirms that the required integer grading for the `sl₂`-action is intrinsic to the cohomological degree (`i` or `p`) and not derived from the Adams weight `q`. The compatibility observed for abelian varieties is an exceptional feature that relies on geometric structures absent for `Spec ℤ`. These structures include the existence of a dual abelian variety `X̂` and a normalized Poincaré bundle on `X × X̂`, which are essential for the non-axiomatic Fourier-Mukai transform construction of the `sl₂` operators [r29, r59, knnemann1993alefschetzdecomposition pages 4-7, beauville2008theactionof pages 4-8, huybrechts2006fouriermukaitransformsin pages 203-207]. Therefore, the literature does not contain a construction for `Spec ℤ` where the `sl₂` grading operator `H` is identified with the Adams weight operator [r26, r29]. While Arakelov motivic cohomology provides the right shape for a decoupled model, the literature does not yet contain a proven hard-Lefschetz theorem or `sl₂`-triple acting on these groups. Furthermore, a detailed search confirms that these cohomology theories are not formally treated as a tensor product of `p`-graded and `q`-graded modules; instead, geometric operations like the projective bundle formula explicitly couple the two indices (e.g., `H^n(P(E), p) ≅ ⊕_{i=0}^d H_{n-2i}(X, p-i)`), preventing a simple factorization that would allow an `sl₂`-action on `p` to commute trivially with an operator on `q` [r37, r53, holmstrom2010arakelovmotiviccohomology pages 25-28].
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            4.  **Spectrum Mismatch:** The joint spectrum of the `{ψ^n}` operators on K-theory or motivic cohomology of `Spec ℤ` has not been identified with the nontrivial zeros of the Riemann zeta function. The literature connects these Adams-weighted cohomology groups to *special values* or *orders of vanishing* of L-functions (e.g., via Beilinson's conjectures), not to the locations of the zeros themselves. Frameworks that do provide a spectral realization of the zeros, such as the Connes trace formula, use a scaling action on an adèlic space, which has not been identified with the `ψ^n` action on K-theory [r16, connes2602theriemannhypothesis pages 16-19, goncharov2005polylogarithmsregulatorsand pages 1-4].

findings.content: The candidate Lefschetz operator `L` from Arakelov geometry (Route A), defined as the cup-product with the arithmetic first Chern class `L = ∪ĉ₁`, is structurally independent of the spectral operator `T` from the Weil explicit formula framework. The definition of `L` is based on local geometric and metric data, as the class `ĉ₁` of a hermitian line bundle `L̄` explicitly includes a Green's function term `-log∥s∥²` derived from the archimedean metric [r31, iyengar2017theclassicaland pages 47-47]. In contrast, the operator `T` and its von Neumann algebra `W*(T)` are defined by the global analytic data of the Riemann zeta function, namely its set of nontrivial zeros `{γ_ρ}` [r31, r36, herichi2012riemannzerosand pages 21-25, meyer2005onarepresentation pages 1-2, lapidus2015towardsquantizednumber pages 14-15]. This structural distinction is confirmed by a "local variation" argument: varying the hermitian metric `m` on the line bundle results in a non-constant family of Lefschetz operators `L_m`, whereas `T` and `W*(T)` remain invariant. The change in the arithmetic Chern class `ĉ₁(m₁) - ĉ₁(m₀)` is governed by a Bott-Chern secondary class [r38, kuhn2005arithmeticcharacteristicclasses pages 82-85]. An explicit example has been documented for the arithmetic surface `P¹_ℤ` with the line bundle `O(1)`; choosing the Fubini-Study metric `m₀` and a constant rescaling `m₁ = e⁻ᶜ m₀` (for `c ≠ 0`) yields a difference class `ĉ₁(m₁) - ĉ₁(m₀)` that is non-trivial in arithmetic cohomology, as its intersection number with a rational point such as `[0]` is non-zero [r41, kuhn2005arithmeticcharacteristicclasses pages 56-60, tamvakis1996bottchernformsand pages 16-20]. Because `L_m` varies with the metric `m` while `T` is constant, `L` cannot be expressed as a fixed function of `T`, providing strong evidence that `L ∉ W*(T)` and thereby satisfying the crucial independence condition for a non-circular proof of RH [r31, r36, r38, r41].

findings.content: Arakelov geometry (Route A) provides a source of positivity that is independent of the Riemann Hypothesis (RH), offering a potential path around the circularity issues identified in other frameworks. In Arakelov theory, the components of the required Hodge-Riemann structure can be identified with standard arithmetic objects [r32, r39, r42]. The bilinear form `Q` corresponds to the arithmetic intersection pairing followed by an arithmetic degree map, `deg(â ⋅ b̂)`. The Lefschetz operator `L` is the cup-product with the first arithmetic Chern class of an arithmetically ample hermitian line bundle, `L(x) = x ⋅ ĉ₁(H)`. The positivity of this structure is then governed by proven arithmetic Hodge Index theorems, which state that for a primitive arithmetic class `x`, the self-intersection is negative semi-definite: `deg(x² ⋅ ĉ₁(H)ᵈ⁻¹) ≤ 0` [r32, r39, moriwaki1996hodgeindextheorem pages 1-4, moriwaki1998intersectionpairingfor pages 1-3]. This establishes a non-circular source of sign-definiteness. To construct the required positive bilinear form `ω(x,y) = Q(JLx,y)`, the abstract complex operator `J` can be realized as the Weil operator `C` acting on the archimedean differential form components of the arithmetic classes, where `C` acts on a `(p,q)`-form by multiplication with `i^(p-q)` [r42, consani2004noncommutativegeometrydynamics pages 9-11]. With this identification, the required positivity of `ω(x,x)` for primitive classes is achieved by defining it with a negative sign, `ω(x,x) = -deg(C(L(x)) · x)`. In the case of arithmetic divisors (codimension one), the relevant archimedean forms are of type `(1,1)`, so `C` acts as the identity, and the condition becomes `ω(x,x) = -deg((L(x)) · x) ≥ 0`. This directly corresponds to the proven negative semi-definiteness from the arithmetic Hodge Index theorem [r42, moriwaki1998intersectionpairingfor pages 14-16].

findings.content:
A survey of literature in geometric representation theory and quantum field theory reveals the existence of "decoupled compatibility" models that provide a potential resolution to the incompatibility between `sl₂` commutation relations and the conjectured `ℚ`-linear independence of Riemann zero ordinates (see finding `f3.2`). In these models, the Hilbert space is a tensor product `H ⊗ V` (or a direct sum of such spaces). A Lefschetz-type `sl₂` action is realized on the "geometric" factor `H`, while a separate operator, such as a grading or trace operator, acts on the "internal" or "auxiliary" factor `V` [r33]. The commutation between the `sl₂` generators on `H` and the operator on `V` is trivially zero because they act on different tensor factors. This structure is explicitly realized in several contexts:
                                                                                                                                            *   **Relative Hard Lefschetz:** In the framework of Hosono-Saito-Takahashi, the intersection cohomology of certain moduli spaces decomposes into a direct sum of tensor products `I_h ⊗ R_h`, where a "relative" `(sl₂)L` acts on `I_h` and an "absolute" `(sl₂)R` acts on `R_h`. The two actions commute, and this structure is used to define BPS integer invariants by taking a trace over only the auxiliary `R_h` factor [r33, hosono2001relativelefschetzaction pages 4-7, hosono2001relativelefschetzaction pages 7-10].
                                                                                                                                            *   **Conformal Field Theory (CFT):** In 2D CFT, the Hilbert space factorizes into a tensor product of chiral (holomorphic) and anti-chiral (anti-holomorphic) modules, `V_h ⊗ V_h̄`. The Virasoro algebra generators `L_n` act on the first factor while `L̄_n` act on the second, with `[L_n, L̄_m] = 0` [r33, kusuki2412modernapproachto pages 14-17].
                                                                                                                                            This establishes that there is a known mathematical and physical precedent for a structure where a Lefschetz `sl₂` action can coexist with an independent operator (playing the role of the spectral operator `T`) by acting on separate factors of a tensor product space. This allows for compatibility without requiring the spectrum of `T` to have arithmetic progressions [r33].

                                                                                                                                            This establishes that there is a known mathematical and physical precedent for a structure where a Lefschetz `sl₂` action can coexist with an independent operator (playing the role of the spectral operator `T`) by acting on separate factors of a tensor product space. This allows for compatibility without requiring the spectrum of `T` to have arithmetic progressions [r33].

findings.content:
In Arakelov geometry, the construction of the Lefschetz `sl₂`-triple (`L`, `Λ`, `H`) on arithmetic Chow groups is not fully geometric and relies on axiomatic definitions to enforce the required integrality, with a notable exception for abelian varieties [r44, r46, r47, r52, r57, r59, r62, r66].
                                                                                                                                                                        1. **Adjoint Construction Failure vs. Blueprint:** A survey of Arakelov theory literature confirms that the lowering operator `Λ` is generally not constructed as the geometric adjoint of the raising operator `L` with respect to the arithmetic intersection pairing `(a,b) ↦ deg(a·b)`. Instead, `Λ` is often introduced axiomatically via its commutator with `L` to ensure integer grading [r46, iyengar2017theclassicaland pages 44-47, zhang2020standardconjecturesand pages 36-40]. However, the classical (non-arithmetic) theory of Chow correspondences provides a clear blueprint for this construction. In that setting, the lowering operator is standardly realized by the action of the *transposed correspondence* `L^t`, which is defined by swapping the factors of the correspondence defining `L` (`Z^t = flip_*(Z)`). This transposed operator is the formal adjoint of `L` with respect to the intersection pairing (`⟨L(a), b⟩ = ⟨a, L^t(b)⟩`) [r75, knnemann1993alefschetzdecomposition pages 1-4, shimizu2025proofofthe pages 49-51]. The key obstruction is that proving the cohomological lowering operator `Λ` is representable by such an *algebraic* correspondence is the content of the (open) standard conjecture B(X) [r75, mari2010selfcorrespondencesofthe pages 1-4]. Thus, a formal geometric mechanism exists but its realization in the necessary algebraic category for arithmetic geometry is conjectural.
                                                                                                                                                                        2. **Hodge Star Construction Failure:** There is no established "global arithmetic Hodge star" operator `*_{ar}` on arithmetic Chow groups that would allow `Λ` to be defined by the formula `Λ = c · *_{ar} L *_{ar}⁻¹`. While classical (non-arithmetic) Hodge star operators exist on the complex-analytic part of an arithmetic variety, a global operator acting on both finite and archimedean components has not been constructed [r47, kresch2001standardconjecturesfor pages 1-3].
                                                                                                                                                                        3. **Axiomatic Definition:** In practice, the lowering operator `Λ` and the grading operator `H` are often introduced axiomatically. For instance, in Lefschetz-module formalisms, `Λ` is defined as the operator that satisfies the commutation relation `[L, Λ] = H`, where `H` is required to act on the `p`-th graded component by multiplication with the integer `2p - d`. This ensures the integer grading required for a lowest-weight representation, but does so by definition rather than by geometric construction [r44, r46, zhang2020standardconjecturesand pages 36-40].
                                                                                                                                                                        4. **Exception for Abelian Varieties and its limitations:** In the specific case of an arithmetic abelian variety, a non-axiomatic, fully geometric construction of the `sl₂`-triple exists [r52, r57]. The lowering operator `A` is defined via the Fourier-Mukai transform, whose kernel is the Poincaré bundle, while `L` is the cup-product with a polarization. These operators satisfy the `sl₂` relations, and the adjointness `⟨La, b⟩ = ⟨a, Ab⟩` is a consequence of a general theorem for correspondences and their transposes under the height pairing [r57, kunnemann1996higherpicardvarieties pages 4-8, beauville2008theactionof pages 8-12]. However, this construction is specific to abelian varieties and cannot be applied to `Spec ℤ`. A systematic review confirms that the construction fundamentally requires a set of geometric properties that `Spec ℤ` lacks, including: being a projective abelian scheme with a group structure, the existence of a dual abelian variety `X̂` representing the `Pic⁰` functor, and the existence of a normalized Poincaré bundle on `X × X̂` to serve as the transform's kernel [r59, huybrechts2006fouriermukaitransformsin pages 203-207, polishchuk1997analogueofweil pages 1-3]. A subsequent detailed survey of more advanced integral transforms (e.g., spherical DG-functors, kernels on stacks) confirmed that while the categorical machinery for such transforms exists, and classifying stacks like `BGL₁` are natural candidates for a "dual" object to `Spec ℤ`, no theorem has been published that uses a kernel on `Spec ℤ × BGL₁` (or a similar construction) to produce an `sl₂`-triple on the motivic cohomology of `Spec ℤ` [r62, anno2017sphericaldgfunctors pages 2-4, benzvi2017integraltransformsfor pages 1-2, donagi2008torusfibrationsgerbes pages 4-7].
                                                                                                                                                                        5. **Partial Success with Derived Hecke/Correspondence Algebras:** The search for geometric operators has yielded partial success in the framework of derived Hecke algebras and correspondence algebras. Literature on this topic shows that degree-shifting operators on arithmetic cohomology are naturally realized as pull-cup-push correspondences. For example, in the `PGL₂` setting, an explicit degree-+1 derived Hecke operator `T_{q,α}` has been constructed by pulling back cohomology, cupping with a class in `H¹`, and pushing forward [r66, venkatesh2019derivedheckealgebra pages 5-8]. This provides a concrete geometric source for a raising operator (`e` or `L`). However, the literature does not contain a full `sl₂` structure derived from this source. A targeted survey of the derived Hecke algebra literature confirms that while various geometric and Galois-theoretic duality pairings are defined, the lowering operator `Λ` (or `f`) is not constructed as the formal adjoint of the raising operator `L` with respect to a Poincaré-type pairing on arithmetic manifold cohomology [r71]. While adjointness relations exist for other types of degree-shifting operators in related contexts [r71, prasanna2021automorphiccohomologymotivic pages 32-35], the assembly of a complete (`e`,`f`,`h`) triple from derived Hecke operators remains an open problem [r66, r71].

findings.content:
In certain arithmetic-geometric contexts, there is a proven mechanism for compatibility between a Lefschetz `sl₂` action and spectral operators (like Hecke operators `T_n`), realizing a form of "decoupled compatibility." Specifically, for Shimura varieties and modular curves, the commutation `[L, T_n] = 0` is established or expected via the same underlying geometric principle [r60, r64, r67].
                                                                                                                                                                            1.  **Operator Definitions:** In both classical and arithmetic settings, the operators are defined by geometric correspondences. Hecke operators `T_n` act via a push-pull formula (`T_n = (p₂)∗ ∘ (p₁)∗` or an arithmetic analogue) along a Hecke correspondence [r64, r67, milne1992thepointson pages 79-82, menares2011correspondencesinarakelov pages 18-22]. The Lefschetz operator `L` is the cup product with the Chern class of a canonical (hermitian) automorphic line bundle `ω`, such as the Hodge bundle (`L = ∪ c₁(ω)`) or its arithmetic counterpart (`L(x) = x ⋅ ĉ₁(ω̄)`) [r64, r67, dat2014lefschetzoperatorand pages 1-5, gil2007cohomologicalarithmeticchow pages 184-188].
                                                                                                                                                                            2.  **Commutation Mechanism:** The commutation `[L, T_n] = 0` follows from the projection formula combined with the geometric fact that the automorphic line bundle `ω` is invariant under the Hecke correspondence. This invariance has been established for canonical metrics (e.g., Petersson or good log-singular metrics) on automorphic bundles over Shimura varieties [r72]. Such metrics are constructed to be invariant under the group actions that define Hecke correspondences. This results in an isometry of hermitian line bundles between the pullbacks along the two projections of the correspondence space, `p₁∗ω̄ ≅ p₂∗ω̄` [r64, r67, r72, porta2209generalisedthetaoperators pages 14-17, ichino2021periodsofquaternionic pages 17-19, hormann2014thegeometricand pages 55-59]. In the arithmetic Arakelov setting, this preservation of the hermitian metric under the correspondence implies commutation of the associated operators on arithmetic Chow groups [r67].
                                                                                                                                                                            3.  **Compatibility Implication:** This proven commutation provides a concrete example of the "decoupled compatibility" model (see `f12`) in both classical (non-arithmetic) and arithmetic (Arakelov) geometry. The `sl₂` action (generated by `L`) acts on the cohomological degree, while the Hecke operators `T_n` act on the same space, and the two sets of operators commute. This allows the `sl₂` structure to coexist with the action of spectral operators (`T_n`) without imposing arithmetic constraints (like arithmetic progressions) on the Hecke spectrum.

    <title>Arakelov-λ-Ring Blueprint for K_geom: Independent Lefschetz Class and Non-Circular Positivity with Open sl<sub>2</sub> Realization</title>
    <summary>Arakelov geometry supplies a canonical candidate for the Lefschetz operator L as cup-product with an arithmetic first Chern class, and a bilinear form Q given by arithmetic intersection followed by degree, together yielding a Hodge-Riemann sign via proven arithmetic Hodge index theorems. A metric-variation argument establishes structural independence of L from the spectral operator T: changing the hermitian metric alters L while T, determined by the zero set, remains fixed, implying L ∉ W*(T). λ-ring/Adams operations contribute an intrinsic integer grading, and in Arakelov motivic cohomology L shifts the Adams weight compatibly; however, the sl<sub>2</sub> grading operator aligns with cohomological degree, not Adams weight, and the absolute surface itself is not yet constructed. A geometric Λ (lowering operator) is missing in general (except for abelian varieties), but compatibility patterns such as [L, Hecke]=0 and known tensor-factor decouplings indicate a viable path to coexistence with spectral operators without violating linear-independence constraints.</summary>
    <background>Route A seeks K_geom/SURF inside Arakelov-type or 𝔽<sub>1</sub>/λ-ring architectures, aiming for a cohomology with integer grading, a geometric Lefschetz class [Δ], and a positive Hodge-Riemann form independent of RH. Arithmetic Chow and motivic theories deliver canonical intersection pairings and arithmetic Chern classes, while λ-ring structures furnish Adams operations ψ<sup>n</sup> that generate an integer weight decomposition. The open challenge is to assemble a true sl<sub>2</sub>-triple (L,Λ,H) acting on the appropriate arithmetic cohomology of an "absolute surface," with Λ realized geometrically and H giving an integer, lowest-weight grading. This group synthesizes the pieces that already satisfy core gates (independence, positivity) and identifies what remains open.</background>
    <content>
    Arakelov theory identifies the natural bilinear form Q as the arithmetic intersection pairing followed by degree, and the Lefschetz operator as L(x)=x⋅ĉ<sub>1</sub>(H̄) for an arithmetically ample hermitian line bundle. The complex structure J can be implemented by the archimedean Weil operator C acting on (p,q)-forms, so that defining ω(x,y)=−deg(C(L(x))·y) produces positivity on primitive classes. This is a non-circular source of sign-definiteness: for arithmetic divisors, the relevant (1,1)-form component makes C act as the identity and the arithmetic Hodge Index theorem gives −deg((L(x))·x)≥0 on primitives. Thus, Arakelov geometry supplies G4-type positivity without presupposing RH.

    Independence of L from T is certified by metric variation. The arithmetic first Chern class depends on the archimedean metric via a Green's function term −log∥s∥<sup>2</sup>, so varying the metric m deforms L to L_m while the spectral operator T, defined by the set of nontrivial zeros {γ_ρ}, is invariant. The difference ĉ<sub>1</sub>(m<sub>1</sub>)−ĉ<sub>1</sub>(m<sub>0</sub>) is a Bott-Chern secondary class and for P<sup>1</sup>_ℤ with O(1), the rescaling m<sub>1</sub>=e<sup>−c</sup>m<sub>0</sub> yields a nontrivial class whose intersection with a rational point like [0] is nonzero. Because W*(T) is fixed by {γ_ρ} and L_m varies with m, this furnishes strong evidence that L ∉ W*(T), passing the independence gate.

    λ-ring structures provide an intrinsic integer grading via Adams operations ψ<sup>n</sup>, which act diagonally with eigenvalues n<sup>j</sup> on weight-j components after rationalization. In Arakelov motivic cohomology, L=∪ĉ<sub>1</sub> shifts degree by +2 and Adams weight by +1, supplying a compatible bigrading and a concrete raising operator. However, the grading operator H in an sl<sub>2</sub>-triple is determined by cohomological degree (e.g., 2p−d), not by Adams weight, so identifying H with the Adams-weight operator is obstructed. Moreover, the sought "absolute surface" Spec ℤx<sub>𝔽<sub>1</sub></sub>Spec ℤ with a diagonal class [Δ] remains unconstructed, and adjacent 𝔽<sub>1</sub>-models that give spectral spaces (e.g., H<sup>1</sup>(P<sup>1</sup>_{𝔽<sub>1</sub>},Ω)) do not provide the required sl<sub>2</sub>-triple; related metaplectic/prolate sl<sub>2</sub> realizations have continuous spectra for the scaling generator, not the discrete lowest-weight grading needed.

    The lowering operator Λ is not generally realized as a geometric adjoint of L in arithmetic settings; instead, Λ and H are typically imposed axiomatically to secure integrality. A notable exception occurs for abelian varieties, where a Fourier-Mukai mechanism (with Poincaré bundle kernel) yields a bona fide geometric sl<sub>2</sub> with adjointness, but this relies on structures absent for Spec ℤ. Partial geometric progress exists in derived Hecke/correspondence algebras, where explicit degree-raising operators are constructed by pull-cup-push, though a full (e,f,h) triple is not yet assembled. Crucially, decoupled compatibility is available: on Shimura-type geometries, L commutes with Hecke operators because the automorphic hermitian metric is preserved under Hecke correspondences, and, more generally, tensor-factor models show how an sl<sub>2</sub> on one factor can coexist with an independent spectral action on another. Together, these features chart a rigorous blueprint that passes independence and non-circular positivity, isolates the missing Λ/[Δ]/surface pieces, and suggests a concrete path to G1-G4 once the absolute surface is built.</content>
